maintained PHI unencrypted (createTables.sql); Authorization forms/flows don’t add confidentiality controls beyond RBAC/audit, so created/received data remains unencrypted. - Digital signature/auth checks are weak (name-based), offering little protection if traffic or storage is compromised.
records release is separate form/JSP/table (RecordsReleaseForm.java; requestRecordsRelease.jsp); no explicit block on compound auth.

key required elements are missing. The records release form (`model/old/beans/forms/RecordsReleaseForm.java`, `WebRoot/auth/patient/requestRecordsRelease.jsp`) captures description/purpose and parties, and has a simple “digitalSignature” checkbox, but there’s no expiration date/event field, no personal-representative authority field, and the signature check is just a name match (`server/RecordsReleaseServlet.java` → `checkCredentials`). Validator (`model/old/validate/RecordsReleaseFormValidator.java`) enforces required fields but none of these missing elements. Without expiration and a robust signature/authority capture, the HIPAA authorization criteria aren’t met.

the authorization flow never informs users of a right to revoke or how to do so. The form bean has no revoke-related fields (`model/old/beans/forms/RecordsReleaseForm.java`), the JSP text lacks any revocation statement (`WebRoot/auth/patient/requestRecordsRelease.jsp`), the validator doesn’t check for it (`model/old/validate/RecordsReleaseFormValidator.java`), and the servlet/action don’t enforce or store revocation info (`server/RecordsReleaseServlet.java`, `action/RequestRecordsReleaseAction.java`). There’s no workflow to capture, persist, or honor revocation.

The authorization form/JSP text (`WebRoot/auth/patient/requestRecordsRelease.jsp`) has no conditioning statement about treatment/payment/enrollment, and no redisclosure warning. The form bean (`model/old/beans/forms/RecordsReleaseForm.java`), validator (`model/old/validate/RecordsReleaseFormValidator.java`), and servlet/action (`server/RecordsReleaseServlet.java`, `action/RequestRecordsReleaseAction.java`) don’t hold or enforce those statements.

requestRecordsRelease.jsp show a short, plain statement about releasing records (and a variant for dependents). The form bean RecordsReleaseForm.java and validator RecordsReleaseFormValidator.java don’t enforce readability; they just require a checkbox/signature.

456bc583-cfbd-43c6-848c-f85006ad6e1f.reasoning: There’s basic auditing (TransactionLogger → `transactionlog` table in `sql/createTables.sql`), but no code or tooling for risk assessments: no threat models, vulnerability scans, encryption controls, or documented risk register/mitigations. To improve: add formal risk assessment workflow (e.g., STRIDE/LINDDUN), scheduled vuln scans/static analysis, inventory of ePHI data flows and assets, encryption/key management for storage/backups, HTTPS/TLS enforcement, least-privilege DB access, and monitoring/alerting with documented remediation steps.

99234c2d-5838-4f45-9a84-1eef505b4126.reasoning: Sessions auto-expire after a configured inactivity window and inactive logouts are recorded, meeting the session-timeout security requirement.

The system can deactivate patient accounts and mark deactivation timestamps; searches can include deactivated users, and deactivation removes representative links. However, there is no explicit workforce offboarding workflow, role revocation, or automatic disabling of all user types beyond patient deactivation, so termination coverage is partial.

Access to ePHI is granted via role-based accounts and login authentication. The system enforces authentication with salted/hashed passwords and tracks login failures/timeouts, and authorization is role-based (patients, HCPs, admins, etc.) through DAO and role checks.

Patient identifiers (MIDs) are openly exposed in search results and URLs, and access redirection uses raw MID parameters without masking, so access controls aren’t enforced at the UI/transaction level per policy.

The system records user roles and logs access events, providing a basic record of who accessed what, but it lacks explicit documentation/attestation workflows capturing authorization rationale or approvals for each access right, so documentation of rights is partial.

Access logs can be reviewed for a user (including date filtering and role-based views), enabling periodic review of who accessed ePHI. However, there’s no explicit scheduled review workflow or policy enforcement beyond ad-hoc log viewing.

The system supports modifying user credentials and marking patients as deactivated, and role validation occurs during password reset, but there is no comprehensive workflow to modify access rights/roles across all workforce members, nor approval/audit for role changes beyond basic logging.

A privacy policy page exists, but there is no explicit notice informing individuals of the right to request and receive a paper copy, nor functionality to request/fulfill such copies. The requirement is only partially represented by a general privacy policy link.

The privacy policy page exists but does not address providing the notice no later than first service delivery or after emergency treatment; there is no workflow or content ensuring timing of notice delivery.

The site includes a prominent footer/nav link to the privacy policy on all pages and the policy is available electronically, but there’s no explicit requirement that the site provides customer service/benefits info or highlights “prominently posts” beyond a standard footer link; no separate prominence or homepage posting is enforced.

0133784d-c032-4518-97c6-b6b58fe671aa.reasoning: Positive controls: role-based access is enforced (web.xml), user roles checked (AuthDAO.getUserRole), session role/MID management (SessionUtils), and a transaction/audit system logs many PHI accesses (TransactionDAO / TransactionLogger; many actions call logTransaction). Weaknesses: some code paths intentionally bypass per-call logging (ViewMyRemoteMonitoringListAction.getPatientDataWithoutLogging used by WebRoot/auth/uap/getPatientMonitorList.jsp), and a high-level privacyPolicy.jsp relies on "professional judgment" rather than strict enforcement. Public servlet mappings (e.g., /charts/*) could expose PHI if misused. These gaps risk incidental PHI disclosures occurring outside documented permitted disclosures or without per-access audit.

the app has role-based access and audit logging (so permitted disclosures can be tracked) but there are inconsistent code paths that display PHI without per-access authorization/audit or minimum-necessary filtering. That means incidental disclosures could occur when the underlying access isn’t strictly justified or recorded.

Patients can view their own records via patient-facing pages (protected by role-based rules), and can view their access log — so the system provides a direct access path for individuals to obtain PHI.; The system also supports formal record-release requests to third parties (RecordsReleaseServlet) and logs release-related transactions.; Caveats: the records-release flow is “pending” (manual approval workflow) and its digital-signature check is trivial (digitalSig.equals(loggedInName)), and there are isolated places where PHI access bypasses per-access logging (see earlier NFR). Those weaken assurance that a request → disclosure path is robust/auditable.

The app provides a patient-facing accounting UI (viewAccessLog.jsp + ViewMyAccessLogAction) that queries transaction logs (TransactionDAO.getAllRecordAccesses/getRecordAccesses) and shows who/when via TransactionBean/TransactionType. That meets the basic “accounting on request” need.; Shortcomings: some PHI accesses bypass per-access logging (e.g., ViewMyRemoteMonitoringListAction.getPatientDataWithoutLogging / getPatientMonitorList.jsp), transaction entries lack a structured “purpose” field (only transactionType/addedInfo), and there’s no explicit retention policy in code. Some accesses bypass logging (ViewMyRemoteMonitoringListAction.getPatientDataWithoutLogging / getPatientMonitorList.jsp). Logs store transactionType/addedInfo but no structured “purpose” field (TransactionBean/TransactionType). Only a JSP/UI exists (no documented API endpoint) and some release checks are weak.

No code was found implementing patient-requested restrictions or consent management. Searches for "restriction", "restrict", and "consent" returned no matches in source files. The only related functionality is RecordsReleaseServlet (requesting release to third parties) and the privacy policy page — those support requesting releases, not requesting contractual restrictions on use/disclosure for operations. iTrust has role-based access and some manual release workflow, but lacks: - A patient-facing UI or API to submit restriction requests for health care operations. - Backend storage/tracking of granted/denied restrictions (no DB tables or DAO methods found). - Enforcement logic that checks and honors such restrictions before displaying or disclosing PHI.

No code paths for "amend", "amendment", or "correction" were found in the codebase — grep returned no matches. The only related patient-initiated workflow is for records release (requestRecordsRelease), which supports requesting disclosure to third parties, not requesting amendments to the patient's own PHI. - While patients can edit some personal/basic health info via patient pages (PATIENT_HEALTH_INFORMATION_EDIT exists in TransactionType), there is no formal "request an amendment" workflow that:   - Accepts an amendment request,   - Stores the request and its status,  - Routes it for review/approval, and  - Records the outcome (granted/denied) and any appended statements — all required by the amendment rule.- No DAO/table, servlet, action, or JSP implements amendment request handling or enforcement.

There is no amendment-request/acceptance feature in the codebase, so nothing records or links an “accepted amendment” to specific record entries. Patients can view/edit some basic info (patient pages), but no formal workflow exists to submit, approve, or record amendments and the exact record IDs/fields changed.

no amendment workflow exists to accept and record patient-requested changes, so nothing appends or links accepted amendments to original records. The app allows direct edits to some patient data, but there’s no mechanism to record an amendment request, approval, or tie an “accepted amendment” to the original record entry.

No code supports inbound amendment notifications (no matches for key integration terms: "amend", "amendment", "fhir", "webhook", "mllp", "soap" etc.). Only unrelated listeners (SessionTimeoutListener) exist. Therefore the system cannot receive external amendment notifications or automatically apply updates to the local designated record set.

No amendment-request/processing feature exists. Repo contains no "amend"/"amendment" code, no DB/storage for amendment requests or processing metadata, and no place that records titles/offices responsible for handling amendments. Transaction logging exists but not for amendment workflows or processor titles.

The system requires patient authorization for record releases (UI/form + servlet + DAO + digital-signature check) and enforces role-based access controls elsewhere. However the "digital signature" check is rudimentary (string equality with logged-in name) and enforcement for other PHI disclosures is not shown as a strict, auditable authorization/consent-enforcement pipeline.

Release requests capture scope details (recipient doctor/hospital, justification) and are stored, but there is no clear code path shown that enforces scope at time-of-disclosure (no release/transfer engine that enforces recipient/hospital restrictions or checks before delivering data). Without an approval/fulfillment enforcement step, scope-exceeding disclosures could occur.

Privacy policy explicitly states written permission is optional and the system collects/stores PIHI for treatment and operations; records-release and signature form exist to record consent/authorization when needed.
